messages.content: hy
Hi! What are you trying to do in Obsidian—take notes, organize a vault, set up templates, or something else?
im writhing blog posts

What kind of workflow do you want in Obsidian for writing blog posts?

A solid default setup is:

1) **Folder structure**
- `Blog/Posts/` (drafts + published)
- `Blog/Assets/` (images, attachments)
- `Blog/Meta/` (ideas, outlines, research)

2) **A post template (with frontmatter)**
Create a template note like `Templates/Blog Post.md`:

```md
---
title: "{{title}}"
date: {{date:YYYY-MM-DD}}
status: draft
tags: [blog]
slug: 
summary: 
---

# {{title}}

## TL;DR
- 

## Outline
- 

## Draft
Write here...

## Notes / Sources
- 
```

3) **Statuses to track**
Use `status: idea | draft | editing | scheduled | published`.

If you tell me:
- where you publish (WordPress, Ghost, Medium, static site like Hugo/Jekyll),
- whether you want Markdown export,
- and how you want to track progress (Kanban? Dataview table?),

…I can tailor the exact templates + views for your setup.
i am using hugo i opened the content folder inside obsidian as we are talking now

Good setup. If your Obsidian vault is the Hugo `content/` folder, the main thing is to make Obsidian produce Hugo-friendly Markdown (front matter, links, images).

### 1) Use Hugo-style front matter
Hugo supports YAML/TOML/JSON. Obsidian’s default is YAML, which is fine.

Create a template like `templates/post.md` (or wherever you keep templates in the vault):

```md
---
title: "{{title}}"
date: {{date:YYYY-MM-DDTHH:mm:ss}}
draft: true
tags: []
categories: []
summary: ""
slug: ""
---

## Outline
- 

## Draft
```

In Hugo, posts usually live under `content/posts/`. If you don’t have it, create `posts/` inside your vault.

### 2) Fix Obsidian links so Hugo understands them
Obsidian wiki-links `[[My Post]]` won’t work in Hugo unless you use a plugin/shortcode.

In **Obsidian Settings → Files & Links**:
- **New link format**: *Relative path to file* (recommended)
- **Use Wikilinks**: **OFF**
- **Detect all file extensions**: ON (optional, but helps)

Then you’ll get standard Markdown links like:
```md
[Some note](../notes/some-note.md)
```

### 3) Images/attachments: make Hugo happy
Best practice with Hugo is either:
- **Page bundles**: `content/posts/my-post/index.md` + images in same folder, or
- A global `static/` folder (but you’re currently only in `content/`).

**If you can open the Hugo project root as the vault instead of `content/`, that’s ideal**, because then you can use `static/` and `assets/`.

If you must stay in `content/`, use **page bundles**:

Structure:
```
content/
  posts/
    my-post/
      index.md
      hero.jpg
      diagram.png
```

In Obsidian:
- Settings → Files & Links → **Default location for new attachments**: *Same folder as current file*
- Then reference images in Markdown:
```md
![Hero](hero.jpg)
```

### 4) One-time decision: single file vs bundle
Do you want your posts as:
A) `content/posts/my-post.md` (simpler), or  
B) `content/posts/my-post/index.md` (best for images, recommended)?

Tell me which you prefer and what Hugo theme you use, and I’ll give you an exact working template + a quick “new post” workflow in Obsidian (including a slug generator and folder creation pattern).
i use  the second one [[post]] help me create this template

messages.content:
Create a template file in your vault (e.g. `templates/post.md`) with this content:

```md
---
title: "{{title}}"
date: {{date:YYYY-MM-DDTHH:mm:ss}}
draft: true
tags: []
categories: []
summary: ""
slug: ""
---

## Outline
-

## Draft

```

Then configure Obsidian so it uses it correctly for Hugo page bundles (`content/posts/my-post/index.md`):

1) **Enable Templates core plugin**
- Settings → **Core plugins** → turn on **Templates**

2) **Set your templates folder**
- Settings → **Templates** → **Template folder location** → choose `templates/`

3) **Make attachments stay with the post bundle**
- Settings → **Files & Links** → **Default location for new attachments** → **Same folder as current file**

4) **(Recommended for Hugo) Use Markdown links, not wikilinks**
- Settings → **Files & Links** → **Use Wikilinks** → OFF

### Using it for a new post bundle
- Create folder: `content/posts/my-post/`
- Create file inside it: `index.md`
- Open `index.md` → run **Insert template** → select `post`

If you want, tell me your preferred slug format (e.g. `2026-03-25-my-post` vs `my-post`) and I can adjust the template so `slug` and even the folder name stay consistent.

messages.content:
### `summary` vs `description` (Hugo)
There isn’t a universal, enforced difference—*it depends on your theme/templates*. Common conventions:

- **`summary`**: a short excerpt used in **list pages** (homepage, /posts/, tag pages).  
  - Hugo has built-in summary behavior: it can auto-generate a summary from the content, or you can set it explicitly with `summary` (or by using `<!--more-->` in the Markdown).
- **`description`**: usually used as **SEO meta description** (`<meta name="description">`) for the single post page, and sometimes for social sharing.

Many themes:
- prefer `description` for metadata/SEO
- use `summary` for previews/cards

Your post currently has **`description` but no `summary`**. That’s fine unless your theme expects `summary` for cards. If you want one field only, pick one and adjust the theme—or keep both and set them to the same sentence.

### What is `slug`?
`slug` is the URL-friendly string for your post, e.g.:

- title: “Prison break lesson”
- slug: `prison-break-lesson`
- URL might become: `/posts/prison-break-lesson/`

In Hugo, for **page bundles** like `content/posts/prison-break-lesson/index.md`, Hugo will usually use the **folder name** as the URL by default. Setting `slug` lets you override the URL without renaming the folder.

Useful cases for `slug`:
- You want a shorter/cleaner URL than the folder name
- You rename the title but want the URL to stay the same
- You want to change URL without moving files

### For your specific post
File/folder likely:
- `content/posts/prison-break-lesson/index.md`

Good front matter options:

**Option A (simple, rely on folder name; no slug needed):**
```yaml
---
title: There is more to prison break than just breaking out of the prison
date: 2026-03-23T23:00:00
draft: false
description: The lessons I learned in Prison Break (the series).
tags: [lessons, series]
categories: [Learning]
---
```

**Option B (set both summary + description explicitly):**
```yaml
---
title: There is more to prison break than just breaking out of the prison
date: 2026-03-23T23:00:00
draft: false
summary: The lessons I learned in Prison Break (the series).
description: The lessons I learned in Prison Break (the series).
tags: [lessons, series]
categories: [Learning]
---
```

**Option C (override URL):**
```yaml
---
title: There is more to prison break than just breaking out of the prison
date: 2026-03-23T23:00:00
draft: false
slug: prison-break-lessons
summary: The lessons I learned in Prison Break (the series).
description: The lessons I learned in Prison Break (the series).
tags: [lessons, series]
categories: [Learning]
---
```

### One important fix: your template date syntax
Your template currently has this (it’s malformed):
```yaml
date:
  "{ date:YYYY-MM-DDTHH:mm:ss }":
```

Use this instead:
```yaml
date: {{date:YYYY-MM-DDTHH:mm:ss}}
```

If you tell me your Hugo theme name, I can tell you exactly whether it reads `summary`, `description`, both, or prefers `<!--more-->`.
the name of the theme is congo

Congo (by jpanther) commonly uses **`description`** as the primary “blurb” for SEO and for some list/card displays, and it also supports Hugo’s built-in **summary** mechanism. In practice:

- **`description`** (front matter): best for **meta description / social previews / card text** in Congo.
- **`summary`**: Hugo’s **content excerpt** (auto-generated or controlled via `<!--more-->`). Congo can display it depending on the layout/partials, but you can safely rely on `description` for the short preview text.

### What I recommend for Congo
Use **`description`** as your canonical short pitch, and optionally also control the Hugo summary with `<!--more-->`.

#### Option 1 (simple: just use `description`)
```yaml
---
title: "Prison Break lesson"
date: 2026-03-23T23:00:00
draft: false
description: "What Prison Break teaches about planning, patience, and leverage."
tags: ["lessons", "tv"]
categories: ["Learning"]
---
```

#### Option 2 (best control: `description` + `<!--more-->`)
```md
---
title: "Prison Break lesson"
date: 2026-03-23T23:00:00
draft: false
description: "What Prison Break teaches about planning, patience, and leverage."
tags: ["lessons", "tv"]
categories: ["Learning"]
---

Your opening paragraph that you want people to see in lists.

<!--more-->

The rest of the post continues here...
```

### `slug` for your post
For your bundle at:
`content/posts/prison-break-lesson/index.md`

You usually **don’t need `slug`** because the folder name already becomes the URL:
`/posts/prison-break-lesson/`

Set `slug` only if you want a different URL, e.g.:
```yaml
slug: "prison-break-lessons"
```

### A Congo-friendly post template (page bundle)
Use this as your Obsidian template:

```md
---
title: "{{title}}"
date: {{date:YYYY-MM-DDTHH:mm:ss}}
draft: true
description: ""
tags: []
categories: []
---

Write a strong opening paragraph here.

<!--more-->

## Outline
- 

## Draft
```

If you want, paste your `config.toml/yaml` (or tell me if you enabled Congo “card view” / “article” layout) and I can confirm whether you’ll see `description` or the Hugo summary in your post lists on your site.
So for your current setup, list pages will show Hugo Summary (currently empty for your posts), not Description.

In Congo, list pages *can* show either, depending on the list layout/partials and whether a summary exists. The safe mental model is:

- **If a Hugo `.Summary` exists**, Congo will typically show that.
- **If no summary exists**, it may fall back to **`description`** (or show nothing), depending on the layout.

In your current notes, you have **`description`** but you’re saying list pages show **Summary and it’s empty**. That usually means: **Congo is rendering `.Summary` and your posts don’t have one.**

### How to make Hugo Summary not empty (recommended)
Pick one of these:

#### Option A: Add `<!--more-->` to each post
Put it after the intro paragraph you want as the preview:

```md
Intro paragraph that should appear in lists.

<!--more-->

Rest of the post...
```

This is the most reliable way with Hugo/Congo.

#### Option B: Let Hugo auto-generate it
If you don’t add `<!--more-->`, Hugo will auto-create a summary from the start of the content up to a word limit. If you’re seeing an *empty* summary, common reasons are:
- the post starts with front matter then headings/badges/shortcodes and little plain text
- the theme/list template suppresses it if it doesn’t find usable text

### Alternative: Force the list to use `description`
If you want list pages to always use `description` instead of `.Summary`, you’d override Congo’s list partial (theme override) and change it to reference `.Params.description`. This is doable, but it’s a theme customization (in `layouts/`).

### What I’d do for your Obsidian template (best of both)
Keep `description` for SEO + also ensure summary exists via `<!--more-->`:

```md
---
title: "{{title}}"
date: {{date:YYYY-MM-DDTHH:mm:ss}}
draft: true
description: ""
tags: []
categories: []
---

Write the intro you want to appear on list pages.

<!--more-->

## Draft
```

If you paste one of your post Markdown files (first ~20 lines) and tell me which list layout you’re using in Congo (e.g. “card” vs “list”), I can tell you exactly why `.Summary` is empty and the cleanest fix.
give me examples of summary vs description for this [[Prison break lesson]]

messages.content:
Here are clear, Congo/Hugo-friendly examples of **`description`** vs **summary** for your post.

## 1) Example `description` (front matter)
Think of `description` as the **one-sentence pitch** for SEO + social previews. It should be polished and specific.

Good options for your post:

**Description option A (direct):**
- `description: "Lessons from T-Bag in Prison Break about refusing victimhood and choosing purpose—even after trauma."`

**Description option B (more neutral/SEO):**
- `description: "A reflection on Prison Break and what T-Bag’s story reveals about trauma, resilience, and the need for purpose."`

**Description option C (short & punchy):**
- `description: "What Prison Break taught me about purpose, resilience, and not staying a victim."`

## 2) Example Summary (what appears on list pages)
In Hugo, *summary is usually content*, not a front matter field (unless your theme is coded to use `Params.summary`). The most reliable way is:

### Summary via `<!--more-->`
Whatever you write **before** `<!--more-->` becomes the summary.

Example using your intro, rewritten a bit for readability:

```md
While watching *Prison Break*, I kept noticing Theodore “T-Bag” Bagwell: brilliant, terrifying, and shaped by an abusive childhood. What stood out wasn’t his crimes—it was how he refused to see himself as a victim, even inside prison and even after escaping.

<!--more-->

(then continue with the rest of your post)
```

That paragraph is your **summary**.

## 3) Putting both together (recommended for Congo)

```md
---
title: There is more to prison break than just breaking out of the prison
date: 2026-03-23T23:00:00
draft: false
description: "Lessons from T-Bag in Prison Break about trauma, resilience, and refusing victimhood."
tags: ["lessons", "series"]
categories: ["Learning"]
---

While watching *Prison Break*, I kept noticing Theodore “T-Bag” Bagwell: brilliant, terrifying, and shaped by an abusive childhood. What stood out wasn’t his crimes—it was how he refused to see himself as a victim, even inside prison and even after escaping.

<!--more-->

While watching prison break, i noticed...
```

### Rule of thumb
- **Description** = “Why should I click?” (1 sentence, clean)
- **Summary** = “What am I getting?” (1–3 sentences, from the start of the post, before `<!--more-->`)
help me format the whole story [[Prison break lesson]] include the description slug and summary

While watching prison break, i noticed Theodoera (T bag), a genius , that turns out to be a criminal doing things that a sane person wouldnt do, but we all have our insanity. 

what lead Theo (T bag) do what he does was his abusive father that uses him. but despite all that T bag didnt give up in prison he lived he refused to be a victim, when they break out of prison he still refuses to give in even though he is free he can just disappear he didnt he went to pursue what he wants.

A woman he met before calling cops on him after she noticed he was a wanted man that commit the crimes he commited.He loved that woman and just wanted her to be him, raised her children together . but ofcourse she couldnt knowing what kind of a man he was once was . and he left her with no harm.

messages.content:
```md
---
title: "There is more to Prison Break than just breaking out of the prison"
date: 2026-03-23T23:00:00
draft: false
slug: "prison-break-lesson"
description: "A reflection on what Prison Break—especially T-Bag’s story—teaches about trauma, resilience, and refusing a victim mindset."
tags: ["lessons", "series"]
categories: ["Learning"]
---

While watching *Prison Break*, I kept noticing Theodore “T-Bag” Bagwell—clearly intelligent, clearly dangerous, and doing things most sane people wouldn’t do. But the part that stayed with me wasn’t only the chaos he caused; it was what his story suggests about pain, purpose, and the choice to keep going.

<!--more-->

What seemed to shape Theo into who he became was his abusive father—someone who used him and damaged him early. That kind of beginning can easily become an excuse to stop trying, to surrender to anger, or to live permanently as a victim of what was done to you.

But T-Bag refused that role.

Even in prison, he didn’t give up. He adapted. He survived. And when they finally broke out, he still didn’t “disappear” the way many people would if they were free. He could have vanished and never been seen again, but instead he continued chasing what he wanted—almost like he needed a reason to keep moving.

There’s a moment where he meets a woman, and she realizes he’s a wanted man. She calls the cops. What stood out to me is that he doesn’t harm her. He actually seems to love her, and in his own broken way he wants a real life: to be with her and help raise her children. Of course, she can’t accept him once she knows what he’s done—and she shouldn’t have to.

So he leaves her without hurting her.

And then he keeps going—looking for something that feels like purpose, something that makes life worth continuing. In the end, the lesson I pulled from this isn’t that his actions were justified (they weren’t). It’s that even someone deeply damaged can still refuse to be only a product of their past.

He refuses to be a victim—of prison, of his childhood, or even of his own mind.
```

messages.content:
Here’s what each part of your `[[post]]` template is doing, how Hugo (Congo) uses it, and how you should write inside it.

## 1) Front matter (the `--- ... ---` block)
This is **metadata** Hugo reads to build URLs, dates, SEO, and list pages.

```md
---
title: "{{title}}"
date:
  "{ date:YYYY-MM-DDTHH:mm:ss }":
draft: true
description: Why should the reader click
tags: []
categories: []
---
```

### What each field means
- **`title`**: The post headline. Obsidian templates use `{{title}}` as a placeholder (depending on your templating tool).
- **`date`**: Publish date/time Hugo uses for ordering and display.
- **`draft`**: If `true`, Hugo won’t publish it unless you build with `-D`.
- **`description`**: Congo uses this a lot for **SEO + preview text/cards**. Make it a strong one-sentence pitch.
- **`tags` / `categories`**: Used for filtering and taxonomy pages.

### Important: your `date` line is wrong
This part:
```yaml
date:
  "{ date:YYYY-MM-DDTHH:mm:ss }":
```
will produce invalid YAML / wrong data for Hugo.

Replace it with a single line:

```md
date: {{date:YYYY-MM-DDTHH:mm:ss}}
```

If you’re using the **core Templates** plugin only, `{{date:...}}` may not auto-fill. In that case either:
- type the date manually, or
- install **Templater** plugin (recommended) and I’ll give you the exact working syntax.

### Add `slug` (since you asked)
For Hugo page bundles, the folder name usually becomes the URL, but adding `slug` makes it explicit:

```md
slug: ""
```

## 2) The opening paragraph + `<!--more-->` (this controls the Summary)
```md
Write a strong opening paragraph here. what the reader is getting out of reading.

<!--more-->
```

In Hugo, everything **before** `<!--more-->` becomes the post **Summary** (`.Summary`).

How you should write it:
- 2–5 sentences max
- say what the post is about + the benefit (what they’ll learn)
- avoid long backstory here

Example:
```md
Prison Break isn’t just a show about escape—it’s a study of planning under pressure. In this post I break down 3 lessons (risk, alliances, and patience) you can apply to real projects and setbacks.

<!--more-->
```

## 3) `# {{title}}` (your H1)
```md
# {{title}}
```

In Hugo, you usually **don’t need** an H1 inside the content because the theme renders the title from front matter. Congo typically shows the title automatically.

You have two choices:
- **Option A (recommended): remove this line** to avoid duplicate titles.
- **Option B: keep it** only if you have disabled title rendering in the theme.

## 4) TL;DR section
```md
## TL;DR
-
```

Use bullets summarizing the takeaway. Good for readers who skim.

Example:
```md
## TL;DR
- A plan beats motivation when things get chaotic.
- Alliances are leverage—choose them intentionally.
- Patience is a strategy, not a personality trait.
```

## 5) Outline section
```md
## Outline 
-
```

This is your structure before you draft. Use headings you’ll later expand.

Example:
```md
## Outline
- Lesson 1: Planning under constraints
- Lesson 2: Social leverage and alliances
- Lesson 3: Patience and timing
- Closing: apply this week
```

## 6) Draft section (the real article body)
```md
## Draft Write here... 
```

Write the post with real headings. Example:

```md
## Draft

### Lesson 1: Planning under constraints
...

### Lesson 2: Alliances are leverage
...

### Lesson 3: Patience and timing
...

### Closing: one small action
...
```

## 7) Notes / Sources
```md
## Notes / Sources
```

Put links, quotes, references, and reminders here. You can delete it before publishing or keep it (Congo won’t show it differently; it’s just content).

---

## A cleaned-up version of your template (with slug + fixed date)
Use this as your `[[post]]` template:

```md
---
title: "{{title}}"
date: {{date:YYYY-MM-DDTHH:mm:ss}}
draft: true
slug: ""
description: ""
tags: []
categories: []
---

Write a strong opening paragraph: topic + promise.

<!--more-->

## TL;DR
- 

## Outline
- 

## Draft
### 

## Notes / Sources
- 
```

If you tell me whether you’re using **Templater** or just Obsidian **Templates**, I’ll adjust the `{{date:...}}` / `{{title}}` placeholders to the exact syntax that will actually auto-fill for you.
im using templater

Use Templater syntax (it’s different from Obsidian core Templates). In Templater you generally use:

- Title (from file name): `<% tp.file.title %>`
- Date/time: `<% tp.date.now("YYYY-MM-DDTHH:mm:ss") %>`

## A Hugo (Congo) page-bundle template for Templater

Create this file: `templates/post.md` (or whatever your templates folder is), then insert it with Templater.

```md
---
title: "<% tp.file.title %>"
date: <%= tp.date.now("YYYY-MM-DDTHH:mm:ss") %>
draft: true
slug: "<% tp.file.slug %>"
description: ""
tags: []
categories: []
---

<%*
/*
  The text above <!--more--> becomes Hugo's .Summary.
  Put 2–5 sentences here that sell the post.
*/
-%>
Write a strong opening paragraph: topic + promise.

<!--more-->

## TL;DR
- 

## Outline
- 

## Draft
### 

## Notes / Sources
- 
```

### Notes about the fields
- **`title`**: comes from the file name (so name your note what you want the post title to be).
- **`date`**: filled automatically at insert time.
- **`slug`**: auto-generated from the file title (lowercase-hyphen style).  
  If you prefer the URL to match the *folder name*, this is usually correct. You can also delete `slug` entirely and rely on the folder name.
- **`description`**: one sentence for SEO + Congo previews.
- **Summary**: write your intro paragraph(s) before `<!--more-->`.

## How you will write content with this template (workflow)
Because you’re using **page bundles**, do this:

1) Create a folder for the post  
`content/posts/prison-break-lesson/`

2) Inside it create `index.md`

3) Open `index.md` → run **Templater: Insert template** → pick `post`

4) Fill in:
- `description` (1 sentence)
- intro paragraph(s) above `<!--more-->` (this becomes the **summary** on list pages)
- then write the rest under `## Draft`

## If you want the template to also create the page-bundle folder automatically
Templater can do that, but it depends on your preferred naming rule:
- folder name from the title slug? (recommended)
- file named always `index.md`?

Tell me what you want the command to do when you create a new post (e.g. “ask for title → create `content/posts/<slug>/index.md` → open it → insert template”), and I’ll give you the exact Templater script.
folder name from the title slug

### Goal
You enter a post title once, and Templater will:
1) generate a slug from it  
2) create `content/posts/<slug>/`  
3) move the current note into that folder as `index.md`  
4) fill in Hugo/Congo front matter + writing sections

This works best if your vault root is **Hugo project root**. If your vault root is the **content** folder, I’ll note the small path change below.

---

## 1) Create the Templater template: `templates/post-bundle.md`

```md
<%*
/**
 * Hugo page bundle creator
 * Creates: content/posts/<slug>/index.md
 */

const title = await tp.system.prompt("Post title");
if (!title) { new Notice("No title provided."); return; }

// slugify
const slug = title
  .toLowerCase()
  .trim()
  .replace(/['"]/g, "")
  .replace(/[^a-z0-9]+/g, "-")
  .replace(/^-+|-+$/g, "");

// If your vault IS the Hugo project root:
const base = `content/posts/${slug}`;

// If your vault IS the Hugo content folder instead, use this:
// const base = `posts/${slug}`;

const targetPath = `${base}/index.md`;

// Ensure folder exists, then move/rename the current file there
await app.vault.createFolder(base).catch(() => {});
await tp.file.move(targetPath);

// Set the note title shown in Obsidian (optional but nice)
await tp.file.rename("index"); // file name becomes index.md (already), safe-ish
// Note: the displayed title will come from front matter anyway.

-%>
---
title: "<% title %>"
date: <%= tp.date.now("YYYY-MM-DDTHH:mm:ss") %>
draft: true
slug: "<% slug %>"
description: ""
tags: []
categories: []
---

Write a strong opening paragraph: topic + promise.

<!--more-->

## TL;DR
- 

## Outline
- 

## Draft
### 

## Notes / Sources
- 
```

### What you’ll edit each time
- `description`: 1 sentence SEO/pitch  
- the intro paragraph above `<!--more-->`: this becomes the **Hugo Summary** on list pages  
- the rest goes under `## Draft`

---

## 2) How to use it (your “new post” flow)

1) Create a new blank note anywhere (e.g. in inbox)  
2) Run **Templater: Insert template** → choose `post-bundle`  
3) Enter the title when prompted  
4) The note will automatically end up at:  
   `content/posts/<slug>/index.md`

---

## 3) One important setting
To keep images with the bundle:
- Settings → **Files & Links** → **Default location for new attachments** → **Same folder as current file**

Then you can do:
```md
![My image](myimage.png)
```
and it will sit next to `index.md`.

---

## Quick check
Is your Obsidian vault opened at the **Hugo project root** or at **`content/` only**?  
If it’s `content/` only, confirm and I’ll tell you which `base = ...` line to use (so paths are correct).

 Template syntax error: Unexpected token '='
eval	@	plugin:templater-obsidian:6
(anonymous)	@	enhance.js:1
oe	@	plugin:templater-obsidian:6
Se	@	plugin:templater-obsidian:7

e.useSelectedItem	@	app.js:1

(anonymous)	@	app.js:1
(anonymous)

The error comes from this line:

```md
date: <%= tp.date.now("YYYY-MM-DDTHH:mm:ss") %>
```

Templater does **not** support `<%=`. It only supports:
- `<% ... %>` (expression output)
- `<%* ... %>` (script block)

So `=` is the “unexpected token”.

## Fix: replace `<%=` with `<%`
Use this corrected template:

```md
<%*
const title = await tp.system.prompt("Post title");
if (!title) { new Notice("No title provided."); return; }

const slug = title
  .toLowerCase()
  .trim()
  .replace(/['"]/g, "")
  .replace(/[^a-z0-9]+/g, "-")
  .replace(/^-+|-+$/g, "");

// If your vault is the Hugo project root:
const base = `content/posts/${slug}`;

// If your vault is the Hugo content folder itself, use:
// const base = `posts/${slug}`;

const targetPath = `${base}/index.md`;

await app.vault.createFolder(base).catch(() => {});
await tp.file.move(targetPath);
-%>
---
title: "<% title %>"
date: <% tp.date.now("YYYY-MM-DDTHH:mm:ss") %>
draft: true
slug: "<% slug %>"
description: ""
tags: []
categories: []
---

Write a strong opening paragraph: topic + promise.

<!--more-->

## TL;DR
- 

## Outline
- 

## Draft
### 

## Notes / Sources
- 
```

## Two quick notes
1) I removed `tp.file.rename("index")` from the previous version—unnecessary (you’re already moving to `index.md`) and it can cause confusion.
2) Make sure you’re inserting this into a *new/empty note* (Templater moves the current file).

## I need one detail to lock it in
Is your Obsidian vault opened at:
- the **Hugo project root** (where `content/` exists), or
- the **`content/` folder only**?

That decides whether `base` should be `content/posts/...` or `posts/...`.

messages.content:
Yes—those two files are exactly the right idea for Hugo + Congo, with a couple of small improvements depending on where they live.

## 1) `content/posts/_index.md` (the “Posts” section page)
This file controls the `/posts/` landing page (title, description, optional intro text). Your content is valid.

Recommended version:

```md
---
title: "Posts"
description: "All blog posts"
---

All my writings.
```

Notes:
- Put this specifically at: **`content/posts/_index.md`**
- `description` here is used by the theme for the section page metadata/heading.

## 2) `content/about/index.md` (the About page as a page bundle)
Your About front matter and content are valid. Congo likes About as a bundle: `content/about/index.md`.

Recommended version (just light grammar + optional extras):

```md
---
title: "About"
date: 2026-03-18
showTableOfContents: false
description: "Hi, I’m Adnan. I build software and document what I learn about self-development, life, and writing."
---

Hi, I’m Adnan.

I build software and document what I learn along the way. I’m focused on self-development and improvement.

This blog shares my life experiences and lessons—writing that entertains or helps people in any way.

## Topics you can expect here
- Anything that comes to mind

You can reach me through the links in the author section.
```

Notes:
- Place it at: **`content/about/index.md`** (not `content/about.md`) if you want the bundle style.
- Adding `description` is optional, but nice for SEO/social previews.

## Quick check
Where are your files located right now?
- `_index.md` should be inside `content/posts/`
- `about` should ideally be a folder: `content/about/index.md`

If you tell me their current paths, I’ll confirm they’ll produce `/posts/` and `/about/` correctly in Congo.

messages.content:
That `_index.md` is correct for Hugo/Congo, as long as it’s saved at:

**`content/posts/_index.md`**

A couple of small tweaks (optional):

1) Add a period (purely style):
```md
All my writings.
```

2) If you want the page to definitely render as a normal page (rarely needed), you can add `draft: false` (Hugo defaults to not-draft if omitted):
```md
---
title: "Posts"
description: "All blog posts"
draft: false
---

All my writings.
```

3) If you want a slightly better “section intro”, you can make it more descriptive:
```md
All my writings—notes, lessons, and long-form posts.
```

But as-is, your file is valid and will control the `/posts/` section page.

linkedNotes.content:
<%\*
/\*\*

- Hugo page bundle creator
- Creates: posts/<slug>/index.md
- Uses create (not move) to avoid ENOENT on Untitled.md
  \*/

const title = await tp.system.prompt("Post title");
if (!title) {
new Notice("No title provided.");
return;
}

const slug = title
.toLowerCase()
.trim()
.replace(/['"]/g, "")
.replace(/[^a-z0-9]+/g, "-")
.replace(/^-+|-+$/g, "");

// If your vault IS the Hugo project root, use:
// const base = `content/posts/${slug}`;

// If your vault IS the Hugo content folder, use:
const base = `posts/${slug}`;

const targetPath = `${base}/index.md`;

await app.vault.createFolder(base).catch(() => {});

const existing = app.vault.getAbstractFileByPath(targetPath);
if (existing) {
new Notice(`Post already exists: ${targetPath}`);
return;
}

const content = `---
title: "${title.replace(/"/g, '\\"')}"
date: ${tp.date.now("YYYY-MM-DDTHH:mm:ss")}
draft: true
slug: "${slug}"
summary: ""
description: ""
tags: []
categories: []

---

Write a strong opening paragraph: topic + promise.

<!--more-->

## Outline

-

## Draft

###

## Notes / Sources

- `;

const file = await app.vault.create(targetPath, content);
await app.workspace.getLeaf(true).openFile(file);
new Notice(`Created ${targetPath}`);
-%>

messages.content:
Yes—this will do what you want **if** your Obsidian vault root is the Hugo **`content/` folder** (so `posts/...` is the correct path). It will:

- prompt for a title
- slugify it
- create `posts/<slug>/`
- create `posts/<slug>/index.md` (page bundle)
- open the new file
- avoid the “Untitled.md move” problem because it uses **create**, not move

### Two fixes I strongly recommend

#### 1) Don’t include `summary: ""` if you’re using `<!--more-->`
In Hugo, summary is better controlled by `<!--more-->`. Leaving `summary` as an empty string can cause some themes/templates to treat it as “set but empty”.

So either remove it, or if you want it, set it to something meaningful. I’d remove it.

#### 2) Your YAML has an extra blank line inside front matter
You have an empty line before `---` closes:

```yaml
categories: []

---
```

That’s not fatal, but keep it clean.

### Cleaned-up version (recommended)
Copy/paste this as your `post-bundle` template:

```md
<%*
/**
 * Hugo page bundle creator
 * Creates: posts/<slug>/index.md   (vault root = Hugo content/)
 */

const title = await tp.system.prompt("Post title");
if (!title) { new Notice("No title provided."); return; }

const slug = title
  .toLowerCase()
  .trim()
  .replace(/['"]/g, "")
  .replace(/[^a-z0-9]+/g, "-")
  .replace(/^-+|-+$/g, "");

const base = `posts/${slug}`;           // use content/posts/... if vault is project root
const targetPath = `${base}/index.md`;

await app.vault.createFolder(base).catch(() => {});

if (app.vault.getAbstractFileByPath(targetPath)) {
  new Notice(`Post already exists: ${targetPath}`);
  return;
}

const safeTitle = title.replace(/"/g, '\\"');

const content = `---
title: "${safeTitle}"
date: ${tp.date.now("YYYY-MM-DDTHH:mm:ss")}
draft: true
slug: "${slug}"
description: ""
tags: []
categories: []
---

Write a strong opening paragraph: topic + promise.

<!--more-->

## TL;DR
- 

## Outline
- 

## Draft
### 

## Notes / Sources
- 
`;

const file = await app.vault.create(targetPath, content);
await app.workspace.getLeaf(true).openFile(file);
new Notice(\`Created \${targetPath}\`);
-%>
```

### How you’ll use it
Run **Templater: Create new note from template** (or insert this into any note—since it creates a new file anyway), enter title, and it will generate the bundle.

### Quick confirmation
Is your vault root actually the **`content/`** folder (so you see `posts/`, `about/`, etc at the top)? If yes, `posts/${slug}` is correct. If you instead see a `content/` directory at the top, swap it to `content/posts/${slug}`.

messages.content: what are other alternatives

### Alternative 1 (simplest): manual bundle creation + insert template
Workflow:
1) Create folder: `posts/<slug>/`
2) Create file: `index.md` inside it
3) Run **Templater: Insert template** (your normal `post` template)

Pros: zero scripting, predictable.  
Cons: manual steps.

---

### Alternative 2: Use **QuickAdd** to create the bundle (best UX)
QuickAdd can:
- prompt for title
- create the folder + `index.md`
- then apply a template

Pros: made for capture flows; no “Untitled” clutter; very reliable.  
Cons: one extra plugin.

If you want, I can give you an exact QuickAdd macro for: `posts/<slug>/index.md` + your template.

---

### Alternative 3: Templater as a **User Script command** (no Untitled file)
Instead of “create note from template”, you run a command that just creates the bundle.

Workflow:
- You run a command like “New Hugo Post”
- it prompts for title
- creates `posts/<slug>/index.md` and opens it

Pros: clean, no temporary note.  
Cons: a bit more setup (a JS file in your scripts folder).

If you want this, tell me where your Templater scripts folder is (or I’ll suggest one) and I’ll provide the script.

---

### Alternative 4: Use Hugo tooling outside Obsidian
- `hugo new posts/<slug>/index.md`

Pros: Hugo-native; exact archetypes; consistent.  
Cons: requires terminal; you still edit in Obsidian afterwards.

---

### Alternative 5: Use an **archetype** + run hugo new (best Hugo consistency)
Set `archetypes/posts/index.md` (or theme-compatible archetype) and run:
- `hugo new posts/<slug>/index.md`

Pros: Hugo handles front matter; no Obsidian templating needed.  
Cons: terminal-based.

---

## Recommendation
If you want the smoothest Obsidian-native workflow: **QuickAdd** (Alternative 2).  
If you want to stay “Templater only” and avoid Untitled files: **User Script command** (Alternative 3).

Tell me which you prefer (QuickAdd vs Templater user script vs hugo new), and confirm whether your vault root is `content/` or the Hugo project root, and I’ll give you a ready-to-copy setup.
use quick add

### 1) Install + enable QuickAdd
- Settings → **Community plugins** → browse → install **QuickAdd**
- Enable it

### 2) Make sure you have a normal post template (Templater)
Create (or keep) a template like: `templates/post.md`

Example (Templater-ready):

```md
---
title: "<% tp.file.title %>"
date: <% tp.date.now("YYYY-MM-DDTHH:mm:ss") %>
draft: true
description: ""
tags: []
categories: []
---

Write a strong opening paragraph (this becomes the summary).

<!--more-->

## Draft
```

Note: In this QuickAdd flow, we’ll set the **folder name** using the slug; the title will usually be your `index` file name unless we pass the title into the template. We’ll handle that below.

---

## 3) Create a QuickAdd “Macro” = New Hugo Post (page bundle)
Open: Settings → **QuickAdd** → **Manage Macros** → **Add Macro**  
Name it: `New Hugo Post (bundle)`

Add these steps in this order:

### Step A — **Input Prompt**
- Add step: **Input prompt**
- Variable name: `title`
- Prompt: `Post title`

### Step B — **Choice: Format** (slugify via built-in formatter)
Add step: **Format**
- Input: `{{VALUE:title}}`
- Formatter: **slugify**
- Save to variable: `slug`

(If your QuickAdd doesn’t show “slugify”, tell me your QuickAdd version and I’ll use a JS step instead.)

### Step C — **Create file**
Add step: **Create file**
- File name: `posts/{{VALUE:slug}}/index`
- (Extension is automatically `.md`)
- If your vault root is Hugo **content/**, this is correct.
  - If your vault is the Hugo project root, use: `content/posts/{{VALUE:slug}}/index`

### Step D — **Template**
Add step: **Template**
- Choose your template file: `templates/post.md`

This applies the template into the newly created `index.md`.

### Step E — (Optional) **Open file**
Some setups auto-open; if not, add “Open file” step (if available), or QuickAdd will focus it after creation.

---

## 4) Fix the title problem (important)
Because the file you create is named `index.md`, Templater’s `<% tp.file.title %>` would become `"index"`.

So you have two good options:

### Option 1 (recommended): store the real title in front matter via QuickAdd
Change your `templates/post.md` to use a QuickAdd variable instead of file title:

```md
---
title: "{{VALUE:title}}"
date: <% tp.date.now("YYYY-MM-DDTHH:mm:ss") %>
draft: true
slug: "{{VALUE:slug}}"
description: ""
tags: []
categories: []
---

Write a strong opening paragraph (this becomes the summary).

<!--more-->

## Draft
```

This works because QuickAdd replaces `{{VALUE:...}}` before Templater runs.

### Option 2: use the folder name only (title still manual)
Keep your template as-is and manually replace `index` in the title. (Not ideal.)

---

## 5) Add it as a command (and hotkey)
- Settings → QuickAdd → **Add Choice**
- Choice type: **Macro**
- Select: `New Hugo Post (bundle)`
Then:
- Settings → **Hotkeys** → assign a hotkey to that QuickAdd choice

---

## Reality check (so paths are right)
At the top of your Obsidian file explorer, do you see:
- `posts/` directly (meaning your vault root is `content/`) → use `posts/{{VALUE:slug}}/index`
or
- a `content/` folder (meaning your vault root is project root) → use `content/posts/{{VALUE:slug}}/index`

Tell me which you see and (if needed) I’ll give you the exact path string to paste.
https://quickadd.obsidian.guide/docs/

Use QuickAdd’s documented “Macro + Capture” approach to create a Hugo page bundle cleanly (no Untitled files). Below is a working setup that matches Hugo + Congo + your “folder name from title slug” requirement.

## A. Prereqs
1) You said your vault is the Hugo **content** folder. That means your posts path is:
- `posts/<slug>/index.md`

(If your vault is instead the Hugo project root, change it to `content/posts/<slug>/index.md` everywhere below.)

2) Make sure your attachments go into the same folder (so images stay inside the bundle):
- Settings → Files & Links → **Default location for new attachments** → *Same folder as current file*

---

## B. Create the template QuickAdd will apply
Create: `templates/post.md`

```md
---
title: "{{VALUE:title}}"
date: <% tp.date.now("YYYY-MM-DDTHH:mm:ss") %>
draft: true
slug: "{{VALUE:slug}}"
description: ""
tags: []
categories: []
---

Write a strong opening paragraph (this becomes the Summary).

<!--more-->

## TL;DR
- 

## Draft
```

Notes:
- `{{VALUE:title}}` and `{{VALUE:slug}}` are filled by QuickAdd.
- The date uses Templater.
- The intro above `<!--more-->` becomes Hugo `.Summary` (what your list pages show).

---

## C. Build the QuickAdd Macro
Settings → **QuickAdd** → **Manage Macros** → **Add Macro**
Name: `New Hugo Post (Bundle)`

Add steps in this order:

### 1) Input Prompt
- Step: **Input Prompt**
- Variable name: `title`
- Prompt text: `Post title`

### 2) Format (slugify)
- Step: **Format**
- Input: `{{VALUE:title}}`
- Formatter: **Slugify** (sometimes listed as `slugify`)
- Output variable: `slug`

If you don’t see “Slugify” in your Format step, tell me—then we’ll do it with a short JS step.

### 3) Create File
- Step: **Create File**
- Path: `posts/{{VALUE:slug}}/index`
  - (QuickAdd will create `index.md`)

Make sure the option to create folders (if shown) is enabled. If QuickAdd doesn’t auto-create folders in your version, we’ll add a JS step to create the folder first.

### 4) Template
- Step: **Template**
- Template file: `templates/post.md`

This injects your front matter + writing sections into the newly created `index.md`.

---

## D. Add it as a QuickAdd “Choice” (so it’s a command)
Settings → QuickAdd → **Choices** → **Add Choice**
- Type: **Macro**
- Select macro: `New Hugo Post (Bundle)`

Then bind a hotkey:
- Settings → **Hotkeys** → search for your QuickAdd choice name → set a shortcut.

---

## E. Test
Run the choice, enter: `Prison break lesson`

It should create and open:
- `posts/prison-break-lesson/index.md`

Front matter will have:
- `title: "Prison break lesson"`
- `slug: "prison-break-lesson"`

---

## If something doesn’t show up in your QuickAdd UI
Reply with a screenshot of:
- your Macro steps list, and
- the “Format” step options (or lack of slugify)

…and I’ll adjust the setup to exactly what your QuickAdd version supports (some versions label steps slightly differently).
is there an alternative to using quick add

Yes—there are good alternatives that don’t require QuickAdd. Here are the most practical ones for Hugo page bundles.

## 1) Use Hugo’s own command + archetypes (most “Hugo-native”)
Set up an archetype, then create bundles via terminal:

**Archetype**
Create `archetypes/posts/index.md` (in your Hugo project root):

```md
---
title: "{{ replace .File.ContentBaseName "-" " " | title }}"
date: {{ .Date }}
draft: true
description: ""
tags: []
categories: []
---

Write a strong opening paragraph (this becomes the Summary).

<!--more-->

## Draft
```

**Create a new post bundle**
```sh
hugo new posts/prison-break-lesson/index.md
```

Pros: perfect Hugo compatibility, no Obsidian/plugin quirks.  
Cons: requires terminal.

---

## 2) Templater “User Script” command (clean, no Untitled note)
Instead of “create note from template”, you run a script from Templater that:
- prompts for title
- creates `posts/<slug>/index.md`
- writes the template content
- opens the file

Pros: one plugin (Templater), smooth, no leftover notes.  
Cons: a bit of one-time setup (scripts folder + script file).

If you want this, tell me whether your vault root is **content/** or project root, and I’ll give you the exact script.

---

## 3) Manual bundle + normal template insert (no automation, very reliable)
Workflow:
1) Create folder `posts/<slug>/`
2) Create `index.md`
3) Run **Templater: Insert template** (your `post` template)

Pros: zero automation issues.  
Cons: manual steps each post.

---

## 4) Use a file/folder-creation plugin (another Obsidian-native option)
Plugins like **Commander** (to chain commands) or **File Tree Alternative** won’t fully solve slug + folder + template alone, but combined with Templater they can.

---

### What I’d recommend
- If you’re comfortable with terminal: **(1) Hugo archetypes + `hugo new`** is the cleanest long-term.
- If you want Obsidian-only but no QuickAdd: **(2) Templater user script** is the best.

Tell me which you prefer (Hugo-native terminal vs Obsidian-only), and confirm: is your vault opened at `content/` or the Hugo project root?
2 the vault is in content/

## Templater user-script solution (vault root = `content/`)
This gives you a command in Obsidian that:
- asks for the post title
- slugifies it
- creates `posts/<slug>/index.md`
- writes front matter + sections (Congo/Hugo-friendly)
- opens the new file  
No “Untitled” notes involved.

---

## 1) Enable Templater “User scripts”
1. Create a folder in your vault, e.g.:
   - `scripts/`
2. Obsidian → Settings → **Templater**
   - **User scripts folder** → select `scripts/`

---

## 2) Create the script file
Create this file:

**`content/scripts/new-hugo-post-bundle.js`**  
(because your vault root is `content/`, the path in Obsidian is just `scripts/new-hugo-post-bundle.js`)

Paste:

```js
module.exports = async (tp) => {
  const title = await tp.system.prompt("Post title");
  if (!title) return;

  const slug = title
    .toLowerCase()
    .trim()
    .replace(/['"]/g, "")
    .replace(/[^a-z0-9]+/g, "-")
    .replace(/^-+|-+$/g, "");

  const folder = `posts/${slug}`;
  const path = `${folder}/index.md`;

  // Create folder if needed
  try { await app.vault.createFolder(folder); } catch (e) {}

  // Abort if exists
  if (app.vault.getAbstractFileByPath(path)) {
    new Notice(`Already exists: ${path}`);
    return;
  }

  const safeTitle = title.replace(/\\/g, "\\\\").replace(/"/g, '\\"');

  const content =
`---
title: "${safeTitle}"
date: ${tp.date.now("YYYY-MM-DDTHH:mm:ss")}
draft: true
slug: "${slug}"
description: ""
tags: []
categories: []
---

Write a strong opening paragraph (this becomes the Summary).

<!--more-->

## TL;DR
- 

## Outline
- 

## Draft
### 

## Notes / Sources
- 
`;

  const file = await app.vault.create(path, content);

  // Open in a new leaf
  await app.workspace.getLeaf(true).openFile(file);

  new Notice(`Created ${path}`);
};
```

---

## 3) Run it from Obsidian
Open Command Palette → search:
- **Templater: Run user script**
Choose: `new-hugo-post-bundle`

It will create:
- `posts/<slug>/index.md`

---

## 4) (Optional) Make it a single command / hotkey
- Settings → **Hotkeys**
- Search: “Templater: Run user script”
- Assign a hotkey to it  
Templater doesn’t always let you bind a hotkey to a specific script directly; if yours can’t, you can use the Command Palette and pick the script, or install **Commander** to bind a specific script.

---

### Writing content with it (Hugo/Congo)
- Fill `description` with 1 clean sentence (SEO/blurb).
- Write 2–5 sentences above `<!--more-->` → that’s the **Summary** on list pages.
- Write the rest under `## Draft`.

If you want, I can tweak the script to also prompt you for `description` and initial tags/categories so you don’t have to fill front matter manually.
